---
title: "Inductive bias, invariance, and equivariance"
---

The unit so far has built the means to train a differentiable program: the
[machine](hardware.qmd) it runs on, the [compiler](xla-jax.qmd) that makes it fast,
the [calculus](differentiation.qmd) of its gradients, the [paradigm](differentiable-programming.qmd)
that uses them, and the [implicit trick](implicit-differentiation.qmd) for the
outputs no formula defines. But all of that is *machinery for searching* — it does
not say *what to search*. The [paradigm chapter](differentiable-programming.qmd#sec-limits)
named this the real problem: choosing the hypothesis class, the family of functions
the optimiser is allowed to pick from. Get it wrong and no amount of compute or
clever differentiation helps.

This closing chapter is about that choice, and it is deliberately a **teaser** — a
map of ideas the rest of the book develops, not a treatise. The organising notion is
**inductive bias**: the structure you build into a hypothesis class to steer it
toward good solutions. Its most principled form comes from **symmetry** — the
programme of *geometric deep learning* — and the two properties it turns on,
**invariance** and **equivariance**, are the running themes of the three units that
follow. By the end you will see why each of geometry, registration, and statistics is,
at heart, the study of a particular inductive bias.

## Hypothesis classes and generalisation {#sec-hypothesis}

Frame the task precisely. There is an unknown process — call it the **referent**
$f^\star$ — that we want to recover from data: a true input–output relationship, or a
distribution. We choose a **hypothesis class** $\mathcal{F}$, a family of
differentiable functions, and search it for an approximation. Two failure modes bound
the choice (@fig-hypothesis).

- **Too narrow.** If $\mathcal{F}$ is too small, it may contain *no* good
  approximation to $f^\star$; the best member $\hat f$ is still far from the truth.
  This is **bias** (underfitting) — the class cannot express what is needed.
- **Too broad.** If $\mathcal{F}$ is too large, *many* of its members fit the
  available (noisy, finite) data equally well, and the one the optimiser lands on,
  $\tilde f$, is likely to have chased the noise. This is **variance**
  (overfitting) — the class expresses too much, and generalises badly.

::: {#fig-hypothesis}
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="The space of all functions contains the hypothesis class as a region; the true process may lie outside it (bias), and noisy fits scatter around the best-in-class member (variance)."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <rect x="30" y="30" width="660" height="200" rx="8" fill="#f4f6f8" stroke="#cfd4d9"/>
  <text x="50" y="52" font-size="12" fill="#888">space of all functions</text>
  <ellipse cx="430" cy="140" rx="200" ry="80" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="430" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">hypothesis class  ℱ</text>
  <!-- truth outside -->
  <text x="150" y="120" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" fill="#b03b4b">★</text>
  <text x="150" y="104" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#b03b4b">f*  (truth)</text>
  <!-- best in class -->
  <circle cx="300" cy="150" r="5" fill="#33373d"/>
  <text x="300" y="172" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">f̂  (best in ℱ)</text>
  <line x1="162" y1="123" x2="293" y2="148" stroke="#b03b4b" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="215" y="128" font-size="11" fill="#b03b4b">bias</text>
  <!-- noisy fits scatter -->
  <g fill="#8a3ffc">
    <circle cx="470" cy="120" r="4"/><circle cx="510" cy="160" r="4"/><circle cx="450" cy="175" r="4"/><circle cx="520" cy="115" r="4"/><circle cx="490" cy="145" r="4"/>
  </g>
  <text x="540" y="150" font-size="12" fill="#8a3ffc">f̃  (noisy fits)</text>
  <text x="490" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#8a3ffc">variance = their spread</text>
</svg>

Hypothesis classes and generalisation. The class $\mathcal{F}$ is a region of
function space. If the truth $f^\star$ lies outside it, even the best member $\hat
f$ is biased; if $\mathcal{F}$ is large, the fits $\tilde f$ obtained from noisy data
scatter widely (variance). A good inductive bias makes $\mathcal{F}$ small enough to
pin the fit yet large enough to contain the truth.
:::

@fig-fit makes the trade concrete on the oldest example there is — fitting a curve
to noisy points with polynomials of growing degree.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fit
#| fig-cap: "Bias and variance, concretely. Noisy samples (grey) of a smooth function (black) fit by polynomials of degree 1 (too narrow a class — underfits, high bias), degree 4 (about right — recovers the shape), and degree 15 (too broad — interpolates the noise, high variance). The hypothesis class is the polynomial degree; choosing it well is choosing an inductive bias."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
truth = lambda t: np.sin(2 * np.pi * t) + 0.3 * t
x = np.sort(rng.uniform(0, 1, 18))
y = truth(x) + rng.normal(0, 0.18, x.size)
xx = np.linspace(0, 1, 400)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.plot(xx, truth(xx), color="k", lw=2, label="truth $f^\\star$")
ax.scatter(x, y, s=22, color="0.6", zorder=3, label="noisy data")
for deg, col, name in [(1, "#b03b4b", "degree 1 (underfit)"),
                       (4, "#2c6a45", "degree 4 (about right)"),
                       (15, "#8a3ffc", "degree 15 (overfit)")]:
    coef = np.polyfit(x, y, deg)
    ax.plot(xx, np.polyval(coef, xx), color=col, lw=2, label=name)
ax.set_ylim(-1.8, 1.8); ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("hypothesis class too small / right / too large")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="lower center", ncol=2)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The lesson is that *more flexibility is not better*. Especially in the small-data
regime, learning succeeds only when the class is the right shape — and shaping it is
the job of inductive bias.

## Inductive bias {#sec-inductive-bias}

The **inductive bias** of a hypothesis class is the set of structural propensities
built into it — the assumptions, explicit or implicit, that make some solutions
easier to reach than others. A useful taxonomy:

- **Implicit vs explicit.** An *implicit* bias is inherent to the architecture or the
  learning algorithm: a linear model is biased toward hyperplane boundaries; a
  convolutional network toward local, translation-respecting features; gradient
  descent itself prefers small-norm solutions. An *explicit* bias is written into the
  objective or the model: a regularisation penalty, a parameter initialisation, a
  hard architectural constraint.
- **Hard vs soft.** A *hard* bias bounds the hypothesis space outright (the class
  simply cannot express certain functions); a *soft* bias merely *prefers* some
  region (a penalty that discourages, but does not forbid).

The most common explicit bias is **regularisation**: augment the objective with a
complexity criterion $c(f)$ and prefer the simplest fit that explains the data. This
is precisely the bridge to the [statistics unit](../statistics/index.qmd), whose
running idea is that **a penalty is a prior is an inductive bias** — a smoothing
penalty $\lambda\,\beta^\top S\beta$ *is* a statement that the truth is smooth, and
the strength $\lambda$ is calibrated from the data by empirical Bayes. It is also the
reading the [three-kernels chapter](three-kernels.qmd#sec-semiring) hinted at:
smoothing a hard, combinatorial operation into a differentiable one is itself a soft
inductive bias, trading exactness for a usable gradient.

But the deepest source of inductive bias is not a penalty. It is **symmetry**.

## Geometric deep learning: symmetry as the principle {#sec-gdl}

A celebrated nineteenth-century idea — Felix Klein's *Erlangen programme* — holds
that a geometry is characterised by its **symmetries**: the transformations that
leave its essential structure unchanged. Euclidean geometry is the study of what
rotations, translations, and reflections preserve; other geometries, other
symmetries. **Geometric deep learning** applies this idea to the design of
differentiable programs: read off the symmetries of the data, and build them into the
hypothesis class as *geometric priors*. If you know that the answer to a question
*cannot* depend on some transformation of the input, you can carve that knowledge
into the model, shrinking the hypothesis class along exactly the directions that do
not matter — a strong, principled, and free inductive bias.

The formal language is **groups** and **representations**. A symmetry of an object is
a transformation that preserves its relevant structure, and the collection of all
such transformations forms a **group** $\mathfrak{G}$ — closed under composition,
with an identity and inverses. The group *acts* on the data; a **representation**
$\rho(g)$ realises the action of a group element $g$ as a concrete (usually linear)
operator on the signal. With that vocabulary, two properties capture almost
everything we want from a symmetry-respecting program.

## Invariance and equivariance {#sec-invariance}

Let $x$ be a signal and $g$ a symmetry acting on it as $\rho(g)x$. A function $f$ is

- **invariant** if the symmetry leaves its output unchanged,
  $$
  f(\rho(g)x) = f(x)\qquad\text{for all }g,
  $$
  and
- **equivariant** if the output transforms *in step* with the input,
  $$
  f(\rho(g)x) = \rho'(g)\,f(x)\qquad\text{for all }g,
  $$
  where $\rho'$ is the representation on the output space.

@fig-inv-equiv draws the distinction. Invariance is what you want of a *decision*:
an image classifier should call a cat a cat wherever it sits in the frame —
translation must not change the label. Equivariance is what you want of a
*feature map*: shift the input image and a good feature map should shift with it, not
scramble. The two compose into the canonical architecture: **convolution is
translation-equivariant** (the convolution of a shifted image is the shifted
convolution of the image), so a stack of convolutions produces features that track
the input, and a final *invariant* pooling collapses them into a translation-invariant
prediction. @fig-conv shows both on a concrete signal.

::: {#fig-inv-equiv}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="Invariance: applying the symmetry before f leaves the output unchanged. Equivariance: applying the symmetry before f equals applying the output representation after f."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <defs>
    <marker id="iea" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse"><path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#55606b"/></marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="190" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">invariance</text>
  <text x="60" y="80" text-anchor="middle">x</text>
  <text x="60" y="160" text-anchor="middle">g·x</text>
  <text x="300" y="80" text-anchor="middle">f(x)</text>
  <text x="300" y="160" text-anchor="middle">f(g·x) = f(x)</text>
  <line x1="80" y1="76" x2="250" y2="76" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#iea)"/><text x="165" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">f</text>
  <line x1="80" y1="156" x2="210" y2="156" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#iea)"/><text x="145" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">f</text>
  <line x1="60" y1="90" x2="60" y2="146" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#iea)"/><text x="48" y="120" text-anchor="end" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">g·</text>
  <text x="190" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#2c6a45">output does not move</text>
  <line x1="430" y1="20" x2="430" y2="210" stroke="#cfd4d9"/>
  <text x="590" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">equivariance</text>
  <text x="470" y="80" text-anchor="middle">x</text>
  <text x="470" y="160" text-anchor="middle">g·x</text>
  <text x="700" y="80" text-anchor="middle">f(x)</text>
  <text x="700" y="160" text-anchor="middle">f(g·x) = ρ(g) f(x)</text>
  <line x1="490" y1="76" x2="660" y2="76" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#iea)"/><text x="575" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">f</text>
  <line x1="490" y1="156" x2="615" y2="156" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#iea)"/><text x="550" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">f</text>
  <line x1="470" y1="90" x2="470" y2="146" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#iea)"/><text x="458" y="120" text-anchor="end" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">g·</text>
  <line x1="700" y1="90" x2="700" y2="146" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#iea)"/><text x="712" y="120" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">ρ(g)·</text>
  <text x="595" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#2c6a45">output moves in step</text>
</svg>

Invariance vs equivariance. For an **invariant** $f$ (left), transforming the input
by $g$ leaves the output fixed — the right-hand nodes coincide. For an
**equivariant** $f$ (right), transforming the input is the same as transforming the
*output* by the corresponding representation $\rho(g)$ — the square commutes.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-conv
#| fig-cap: "Convolution is translation-equivariant; a global pool of it is translation-invariant. Top: a localized input pattern and a shifted copy. Middle: the convolution feature map shifts by exactly the same amount (equivariance) — the operation commutes with translation. Bottom: a global sum of the feature map is (essentially) unchanged by the shift (invariance). Stacking equivariant layers then pooling is how a CNN bakes in 'the answer must not depend on position'."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n = 120
t = np.arange(n)
def pattern(center):                      # a localized bump-and-dip pattern
    s = np.zeros(n)
    s += np.exp(-0.5 * ((t - center) / 4.0) ** 2)
    s -= 0.6 * np.exp(-0.5 * ((t - center - 10) / 3.0) ** 2)
    return s

kernel = np.array([-1.0, -2.0, 0.0, 2.0, 1.0])   # an edge-detecting filter
conv = lambda s: np.convolve(s, kernel, mode="same")

x1, x2 = pattern(40), pattern(75)          # input and its translation
y1, y2 = conv(x1), conv(x2)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(7.4, 5.2), sharex=True)
axes[0].plot(t, x1, color="#3b6fb0", lw=2, label="input")
axes[0].plot(t, x2, color="#d08a2c", lw=2, label="input, shifted +35")
axes[0].set_ylabel("input"); axes[0].legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper right")
axes[0].set_title("equivariance: the feature map shifts with the input")
axes[1].plot(t, y1, color="#3b6fb0", lw=2)
axes[1].plot(t, y2, color="#d08a2c", lw=2)
axes[1].set_ylabel("conv feature")
axes[2].bar(["pool(input)", "pool(shifted)"], [y1.sum(), y2.sum()],
            color=["#3b6fb0", "#d08a2c"])
axes[2].set_ylabel("Σ feature")
axes[2].set_title("invariance: a global pool is (almost) unchanged by the shift")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Why is this such a good inductive bias? Because it removes a whole dimension of
spurious freedom. Without it, a model must *learn* from data that a pattern means the
same thing at every position — burning samples to discover a symmetry you already
know. Building the symmetry in shrinks the effective hypothesis class to functions
that respect it, *without* excluding the truth (which respects it too), so the model
generalises from far less data. That is the central promise of geometric deep
learning, and the reason it matters most exactly where data are scarce.

## How this organises the rest of the book {#sec-units}

Each of the three domain units is, read through this chapter, the study of a
particular symmetry and the inductive bias it licenses. They are not three unrelated
topics; they are three instances of one principle.

| unit | the structure / group | the inductive bias |
|---|---|---|
| [Geometry](../geometry/index.qmd) | **permutation** (vertex relabelling); intrinsic isometry | mesh and graph operators are permutation-**equivariant** — the answer cannot depend on how vertices are numbered; *intrinsic* quantities are **invariant** to the embedding (Theorema Egregium, Gauss–Bonnet) |
| [Registration](../registration/index.qmd) | the nested Lie groups $SE(d)\subset\mathrm{Aff}(d)\subset\mathrm{Diff}(\Omega)$ | transforms are parameterised in the **Lie algebra** and act on images by **pull-back** — the group's representation; surface alignment is $SO(3)$-**equivariant** |
| [Statistics](../statistics/index.qmd) | smoothness / shrinkage (and metric reparametrisation groups) | a penalty *is* a prior *is* an inductive bias; its strength is calibrated by empirical Bayes — regularisation as the soft bias of the whole suite |

The geometry unit opens on the Erlangen programme outright; the registration unit's
whole ladder is a tower of symmetry groups and their representations on images; the
statistics unit's organising equivalence — penalty = prior = variance component =
kernel — *is* the inductive-bias idea, calibrated from data. And every one of them
runs on the primer's machinery: the [hardware](hardware.qmd) it executes on, the
[traced, compiled program](xla-jax.qmd), the [gradients](differentiation.qmd) that
fit it, and the [implicit differentiation](implicit-differentiation.qmd) for its
solver-defined parts.

## What this means for `nitrix`, and where to go next {#sec-nitrix}

`nitrix` is built so that symmetry-respecting differentiable programs are the natural
thing to write. Its mesh and graph reductions are permutation-equivariant by
construction; its registration transforms are parameterised in the Lie algebra of
their group; its statistical fitters carry penalties that *are* priors; and its
relaxations (the [semiring substrate](three-kernels.qmd#sec-semiring)) admit
structured, discrete operations into the differentiable world. The library supplies
primitives that *have the right inductive biases already*, so the programs built on
it inherit them.

This is where the two halves of the whole unit meet, in [its third
thread](index.qmd#sec-thread-structure): **structure is leverage.** Structure in the
*computation* — the memory hierarchy, fusion, sparsity, the choice of semiring — buys
performance; structure in the *data* — the symmetries a model respects — buys
generalisation. They are the same discipline of building known structure into a
program, pointed at speed in the first case and at learning in the second, and the
gradient is what ties them together. With the machinery and the principle both in
hand, the three domain units are where it is put to work — start with whichever
geometry your problem lives in: [discrete geometry](../geometry/index.qmd),
[image registration](../registration/index.qmd), or
[statistical modelling](../statistics/index.qmd).

## Further reading

- Bronstein, M. M., Bruna, J., Cohen, T. & Veličković, P. (2021). "Geometric deep
  learning: grids, groups, graphs, geodesics, and gauges." — the unifying framework
  for symmetry-based inductive bias; the source of the invariance/equivariance
  formalism used here.
- Cohen, T. & Welling, M. (2016). "Group equivariant convolutional networks." *ICML.*
  — equivariance beyond translation, made architectural.
- Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y. & Courville, A. (2016). *Deep Learning*, ch. 5 & 9. —
  the bias–variance trade, capacity, and convolution as an inductive bias.
- Baxter, J. (2000). "A model of inductive bias learning." *JAIR* 12. — a formal
  account of inductive bias and the hypothesis-class picture.
- Mitchell, T. M. (1980). "The need for biases in learning generalizations." — the
  classic argument that learning is impossible without inductive bias.

## See also

- The units this chapter opens, each a study of one inductive bias:
  [discrete geometry](../geometry/index.qmd) (permutation equivariance, intrinsic
  invariance), [image registration](../registration/index.qmd) (Lie-group transforms
  and representations), [statistical modelling](../statistics/index.qmd) (penalty =
  prior = inductive bias).
- The structure-as-leverage thread in the computation: the
  [hardware](hardware.qmd#sec-nitrix) and [XLA/JAX](xla-jax.qmd#sec-nitrix) chapters
  (performance from structure), and the [semiring relaxation](three-kernels.qmd#sec-semiring)
  (admitting discrete structure differentiably).
- Previous chapter: [implicit differentiation](implicit-differentiation.qmd). This is
  the last chapter of the primer; from here, the domain units.
